messages.content: MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054. MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054. - this OSCOLA footnote comes four times in my oscola footnote. Which method should I follow according to official rules?

According to the official rules of OSCOLA, you should use ibid. (short for ibidem) after the first occurrence of a reference that is repeated consecutively. 

In this case, you should use ibid. after the first footnote and include the page number(s), like this:

1 MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054.

2 Ibid. 1054.

3 Ibid.

4 Ibid.

Please note that the page number(s) might vary depending on the specific content of the article.

MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054. MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054. - this OSCOLA footnote comes four times in different places of my article. Which method should I follow according to official rules?

According to the official rules of OSCOLA, when you have multiple references to the same source but not in consecutive footnotes, you should use a short form (see Rule 2.7.2).

For the second and subsequent references to the same source, you should provide a short form citation that includes the author's surname(s), a shortened title (or a cross-reference if appropriate), and a pinpoint reference to the specific page(s) cited. 

For example:

1 MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054.

2 Islam et al, n 1, 1054 (emphasis added).

3 Ibid (emphasis added).

4 Ibid.

In the short form citations, "et al" is used after the first mention of the authors' surnames to indicate that there are additional authors, and "n" refers back to the previous note (in this case, note 1).

Remember to include any necessary page numbers or pinpoint references based on the content you are referring to within the article.

MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054. MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054. - this OSCOLA footnote comes four times in different places of my article but there are other references in the repeatation. Which method should I follow according to official rules?

According to the official rules of OSCOLA, if you have multiple references to the same source, but there are other references interspersed, you should use a shortened citation format for subsequent references.

For subsequent references to the same source, you should provide a short form citation that includes the author's surname(s), a shortened title (or a cross-reference if appropriate), and a pinpoint reference to the specific page(s) cited. 

For example:

1 MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054.

2 Islam et al, n 1, 1054 (emphasis added).

3 MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, n 1.

4 Ibid.

In the short form citations, "et al" is used after the first mention of the authors' surnames to indicate that there are additional authors, and "n" refers back to the previous note (in this case, note 1).

Make sure to include any necessary page numbers or pinpoint references based on the content you are referring to within the article, even if there are other references interspersed.

MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054. MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054. - this OSCOLA footnote comes two times in different places of my article but not consequetively. Which method should I follow according to official rules?

According to the official rules of OSCOLA, when you have multiple references to the same source, but not consecutively, you should provide a short form citation for subsequent references.

For subsequent references to the same source, you should provide a short form citation that includes the author's surname(s), a shortened title (or a cross-reference if appropriate), and a pinpoint reference to the specific page(s) cited.

For example:

1 MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054.

2 Islam et al, n 1, 1054 (emphasis added).

3 MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, n 1, 1054 (emphasis added).

In the short form citations, "et al" is used after the first mention of the authors' surnames to indicate that there are additional authors, and "n" refers back to the previous note (in this case, note 1).

Make sure to include any necessary page numbers or pinpoint references based on the specific content you are referring to within the article, even if there are other references interspersed.
and if they are consequetive?

If the references to the same source are consecutive, you should use the abbreviation "ibid." (short for ibidem) after the first occurrence, instead of repeating the full citation. 

For example:

1 MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Status, challenges and recommendations’ (2019) 235 Journal of Cleaner Production 1054.

2 Ibid.

3 Ibid.

In this case, "ibid." is used to indicate that the subsequent footnote refers to the same source as the immediately preceding one. When using "ibid.," you do not need to repeat the page number(s) unless you are referring to a different page within the same source.

Please note that you should still include any necessary pinpoint references or page numbers based on the specific content you are referring to within the article.

2 Islam et al, n 1, 1054 (emphasis added).

3 MS Islam, MA Ehsan, E Kabir, M Hasanuzzaman, n 1, 1054 (emphasis added). Which should I use of these two?

Apologies for any confusion caused. In this case, since you have already provided a short form citation in footnote 2, you should continue using the short form citation in subsequent footnotes instead of repeating the full citation.

Therefore, you should use the following format for footnote 3:

3 Islam et al, n 1, 1054 (emphasis added).

In this format, "Islam et al" serves as the short form citation referring back to the previous footnote 1. The use of "et al" indicates that there are additional authors. The "n 1" refers back to the previous note, and "1054" provides the pinpoint reference to the specific page(s) cited.

Using this format consistently throughout your document will help maintain clarity and conciseness in your citations.
will n 1 always be constant or it will change according to footnote number?

In the OSCOLA referencing style, "n 1" is a shorthand reference that is used to refer back to the first footnote where the full citation was provided. The "n" stands for "note," and the number refers to the corresponding footnote number. 

As you progress through your document, the footnote numbers will change, so the reference "n 1" will also change accordingly. For example, if you are referring back to the citation provided in footnote 3, you would use "n 3" instead of "n 1."

It is important to update the footnote number in the short form citation to accurately reflect the corresponding full citation. This ensures that readers can easily locate the original source reference.
should I also include  (emphasis added)?

Including "(emphasis added)" is optional and depends on whether you have added emphasis (such as italics or bold) to a specific part of the quoted text from the source material. 

If you have added emphasis to the quoted text in your footnote 2 or 3, then you can include "(emphasis added)" after the page number(s) to indicate that the emphasis in the quotation is your own addition.

For example:

2 Islam et al, n 1, 1054 (emphasis added).

3 Islam et al, n 1, 1054 (emphasis added).

However, if you have not added any emphasis to the quoted text, then there is no need to include "(emphasis added)" in your citations.

MR Islam, MA Rahman, MS Talukder, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Present status and future challenges’ (2019) 148 Resources, Conservation and Recycling 82. - this OSCOLA footnote comes two times in different places of my article but not consecutively. Which method should I follow according to official rules?

According to the official rules of OSCOLA, when you have multiple references to the same source, but not consecutively, you should provide a short form citation for subsequent references.

For subsequent references to the same source, you should provide a short form citation that includes the author's surname(s), a shortened title (or a cross-reference if appropriate), and a pinpoint reference to the specific page(s) cited.

For example:

1 MR Islam, MA Rahman, MS Talukder, ‘E-waste management in Bangladesh: Present status and future challenges’ (2019) 148 Resources, Conservation and Recycling 82.

2 Islam et al, n 1, 82 (emphasis added).

3 MR Islam, MA Rahman, MS Talukder, n 1, 82.

In the short form citations, "et al" is used after the first mention of the authors' surnames to indicate that there are additional authors, and "n" refers back to the previous note (in this case, note 1).

Make sure to include any necessary page numbers or pinpoint references based on the specific content you are referring to within the article, even if there are other references interspersed.

S Khan, MT Rahman, SK Roy, ‘Electronic waste management in Bangladesh: Current scenario and future prospects’ (2019) Journal of Waste Management 1. - this OSCOLA footnote comes two times in different places of my article but not consecutively. Which method should I follow according to official rules?

According to the official rules of OSCOLA, when you have multiple references to the same source, but not consecutively, you should provide a short form citation for subsequent references.

For subsequent references to the same source, you should provide a short form citation that includes the author's surname(s), a shortened title (or a cross-reference if appropriate), and a pinpoint reference to the specific page(s) cited.

For example:

1 S Khan, MT Rahman, SK Roy, ‘Electronic waste management in Bangladesh: Current scenario and future prospects’ (2019) Journal of Waste Management 1.

2 Khan et al, n 1, 1 (emphasis added).

3 S Khan, MT Rahman, SK Roy, n 1, 1.

In the short form citation, "et al" is used after the first mention of the authors' surnames to indicate that there are additional authors, and "n" refers back to the previous note (in this case, note 1).

Make sure to include any necessary page numbers or pinpoint references based on the specific content you are referring to within the article, even if there are other references interspersed.

MT Rahman, MT Uddin, SK Roy, MM Kamal, ‘E-waste Management in Bangladesh: Current Status and Future Challenges’ (2019) 365 Journal of Hazardous Materials 743. - this OSCOLA footnote comes two times in different places of my article consecutively. Which method should I follow according to official rules?

According to the official rules of OSCOLA, when you have multiple consecutive references to the same source, you should use "ibid." (short for ibidem) for the subsequent references.

For example:

1 MT Rahman, MT Uddin, SK Roy, MM Kamal, ‘E-waste Management in Bangladesh: Current Status and Future Challenges’ (2019) 365 Journal of Hazardous Materials 743.

2 Ibid. (emphasis added).

3 Ibid.

In this case, "ibid." is used to indicate that the subsequent footnote refers to the same source as the immediately preceding one. The use of "ibid." means that you do not need to repeat the full citation. However, please note that if you are referencing a different page within the same source, you should indicate the specific page number in the subsequent footnote.

Please make sure to include any necessary pinpoint references or page numbers based on the specific content you are referring to within the article, even if the citations are consecutive.

MT Rahman, MT Uddin, SK Roy, MM Kamal, ‘E-waste Management in Bangladesh: Current Status and Future Challenges’ (2019) 365 Journal of Hazardous Materials 743. - this OSCOLA footnote comes two times in different places of my article but not consecutively. Which method should I follow according to official rules?

Apologies for the confusion in my previous responses. According to the official rules of OSCOLA, when you have multiple references to the same source, but not consecutively, you should provide a short form citation for subsequent references.

For subsequent references to the same source that are not consecutive, you should provide a shortened citation format that includes the author's surname(s), a shortened title (or a cross-reference if appropriate), and a pinpoint reference to the specific page(s) cited.

For example:

1 MT Rahman, MT Uddin, SK Roy, MM Kamal, ‘E-waste Management in Bangladesh: Current Status and Future Challenges’ (2019) 365 Journal of Hazardous Materials 743.

2 Rahman et al, n 1, 743 (emphasis added).

3 Rahman et al, n 1, 743.

In the short form citations, "et al" is used after the first mention of the authors' surnames to indicate that there are additional authors, and "n" refers back to the previous note (in this case, note 1).

Make sure to include any necessary page numbers or pinpoint references based on the specific content you are referring to within the article, even if there are other references interspersed.
